content:
Jules Hedges said:

I'm curious what's your opinion on the option of learning string diagrams before anything else? That's my preferred way to teach, but I didn't learn that way. To pure mathematically minded people I basically define a monoidal category as an algebra of the theory of string diagrams; and to everybody do exactly the same thing but without the fancy words, like Bob does

It's bad.

You think you get them, but until you learn the categories you don't know why they really work. You never really understand the limits of the graphical calculus. So if by any chance the string diagrams people teach you are enough for you to do everything you need, you are fine. But if this is not the case (read: 99% of the times) then you find in a very bad position

So, this is the thing: In string diagrams research people spend way more time tweaking their graphical calculi than using them. It's like playing the harp, you spend 70% of the time tuning it and 30% of the time playing.
Teaching category theory using string diagrams doesn't telly you _anything_ about the tweaking. It's literally like being ablle to play the harp without being able to tune it. It sucks.

For instance, I started playing with combs lately. They really look they do what I want them to do with this categorical broadcasting thing. Still, after 15 minutes I had to bombdive into lens land, because just using combs did not give me any insight about if what I needed to do was allowed or not. In my specific case, I had to "fill a comb" by plugging something between the prongs that wasn't another comb. The graphical calculus didn't really help me understanding if this was possible, and where the resulting thing lived. Then I tried to read the papers, I cried, and then luckily @sarahzrf explained everything to me very clearly (see my point about reading << talking with people), and now (and only now) I can use combs with a minimal degree of confidence.
I think this has more to do with the way string diagrams are presented than with the idea itself.
But we should separate this into another topic, I'd say -- I don't know how that's done (does one have to edit the topic of every individual message?)

I don't know. Let's say that if someone that knows string diagrams well explains something to you using them you believe them and everything seems reasonable, but if you are the one that needs to use them not so much so

If you had a proper definition, you would know the limits. That's what definitions are for in maths. But the most that's done usually is give a reference to Joyal-Street or Kelly-Laplaza that doesn't really have anything to do with anything (una paraculata, per dirla in italiano)...

What I mean is that every time you feel you have to add "something" in your string diagrams you don't know if there's an underlying categorical definition that justifies it. So for instance say that I explain string diagrams to you. Then you need to put feedback loops in. That's "outside" the rules I gave you. Can you do it? You don't know!
On the contrary, if you know what monoidal categories are, you may try to formalize this definition categorically first, and essentially rediscover traced monoidal categories in the process
It's similar with higher dimensional string diagrams. Not knowing the real theory behind them, I'm always in doubt if I can slide/split/merge surfaces :slight_smile:

I actually agree. What I'm thinking is that there's a “market” for diagram-centric definitions of (higher) categories where “diagrams work (up to the extent that they work)” is sort-of tautological, and the tricky part is equivalence with the “traditional” definitions. Similar to how “theorems” of quantum information theory, say Choi-Jamiolkowski, are trivialities if QIT is done natively Bob-style with diagrams.
Which is what I tried to do in my seminar course on categories. I guess that's what I have in mind when I think of “string diagrams-first approaches to categories”.

Then again, what works for us may not work for others and vice versa. I don't deal well with not having proper definitions and with handwaving about the basic objects. That's why I left a physics degree for a maths one after one year. But clearly people finish physics degrees and get quite good at working with handwavy concepts, so why not string diagrams without categories.
I mean, both you and I liked learning from Mac Lane -- I think that puts us in a minority even among category theorists.
